refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle command execution → telemetry return) using network monitoring tools and field trials. Document baseline and stress-test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command delivery.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time packet loss visibility to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert when packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) in the teleoperation protocol stack to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP-C: Establish a dedicated, quality-of-service (QoS) managed network link with traffic prioritization and congestion control for teleoperation traffic.
DP-D: Combine DP-B (FEC/adaptive encoding) with DP-C (QoS management) and add operator-side packet loss monitoring and alerting.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered approach combining forward error correction and adaptive bitrate encoding in the teleoperation protocol, enforce QoS-managed network link with traffic prioritization, and provide real-time packet loss monitoring with operator/safety manager alerts. This DP addresses both prevention (FEC, QoS) and visibility (monitoring/alerting), aligning with high safety criticality.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (FEC/encoding) with network infrastructure (QoS management). This is acceptable because both are necessary to meet the packet loss requirement; however, the coupling introduces dependency on network provisioning and operator training.
Monitoring/alerting logic couples to Safety Services domain (SafetyAlert interface), which is appropriate for high safety criticality.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is not orthogonal but is justified by the safety-critical nature of the requirement. The coupling is managed through clear interface definitions (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and does not create circular dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized; if network conditions vary significantly, packet loss targets may not be achievable.
Assumption: FEC overhead (increased bandwidth) is acceptable within the agreed network capacity.
Risk: QoS management requires network infrastructure support; if customer's network does not support QoS tagging/prioritization, DP-D effectiveness is reduced.
Risk: Operator training required to interpret packet loss alerts; inadequate training could lead to unsafe decisions.
Risk: Adaptive bitrate encoding may introduce latency jitter, affecting operator control responsiveness.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget, (2) QoS mechanism (e.g., DSCP marking, 802.1p tagging), (3) packet loss alert thresholds and escalation logic, (4) fallback behavior if QoS is unavailable. Add a requirement for network characterization and acceptance testing protocol.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol implementation (FEC, adaptive encoding), packet loss monitoring, and operator/safety manager alerting. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (command/telemetry exchange) and Safety Services (alert escalation). It depends on the Network Link for underlying transport.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) flow from operator console into Teleoperation module via Tasking interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state, sensor data, and packet loss metrics flow from Teleoperation module to operator console and monitoring systems via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Packet loss threshold violations and communication degradation alerts flow from Teleoperation module to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport (physical/logical connection) used by Teleoperation module for bidirectional communication; Teleoperation module applies QoS and FEC on top of this link.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse packet loss responsibility across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, Network). Not recommended as primary owner; too broad.

Vehicle module owns on-board equipment control and telemetry generation. Packet loss mitigation (FEC, QoS, monitoring) is not a vehicle-level concern; it is a communication system concern. Assigning here would diffuse responsibility and create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and communication protocol. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. It encapsulates protocol stack, FEC/encoding logic, packet loss monitoring, and alerting. Interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and Network Link. High cohesion, low diffusion. Strongly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert management. Packet loss monitoring and FEC are not safety policy concerns; they are communication quality concerns. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the packet loss mitigation logic. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary because it owns the teleoperation communication protocol stack and has direct responsibility for end-to-end packet loss performance. FEC, adaptive encoding, QoS enforcement, and packet loss monitoring are all communication-layer concerns that naturally belong in Teleoperation. The module interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (command/telemetry), Safety Services (alerts), and Network Link (transport), avoiding diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must implement multiple FEC schemes or support diverse network types. Mitigation: Use pluggable FEC/encoding strategies and abstract network interface.

Risk: Packet loss monitoring adds computational overhead; if Teleoperation runs on resource-constrained operator console hardware, performance may degrade. Mitigation: Offload monitoring to a separate monitoring service or cloud backend.

Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Network Link if QoS configuration is not abstracted. Mitigation: Define a clear Network Link interface contract that specifies QoS capabilities and fallback behavior.

Risk: SafetyAlert escalation logic in Teleoperation may conflict with Safety Services policies if not well-coordinated. Mitigation: Define alert thresholds and escalation rules in collaboration with Safety Services; use SafetyAlert interface as the contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Network Link: Teleoperation should own protocol-level FEC and monitoring, while Network Link owns physical/logical transport and QoS tagging. Define a Network Link interface contract that specifies: (1) supported QoS mechanisms, (2) packet loss measurement capabilities, (3) fallback behavior if QoS unavailable. Add a Teleoperation configuration module to manage FEC scheme selection and alert thresholds, allowing customer customization without code changes.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered FEC + QoS + monitoring) directly addresses the refined FR requirement to maintain packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold. FEC and adaptive encoding reduce packet loss; QoS management prevents congestion-induced loss; monitoring provides visibility for operator and safety manager. The DP is traceable to the FR and covers all sub-FRs (error recovery, visibility, graceful degradation). No logical gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: If FEC/encoding logic is duplicated across operator console and vehicle firmware, maintenance and consistency suffer. Mitigation: Centralize FEC logic in Teleoperation module; Vehicle module uses only standard protocol parsing.

Diffusion Risk 2: If packet loss monitoring is implemented in multiple places (Teleoperation, Safety Services, Network management), data inconsistency and alert storms may occur. Mitigation: Single source of truth in Teleoperation; Safety Services consumes alerts via SafetyAlert interface.

Diffusion Risk 3: QoS configuration scattered across network infrastructure, Teleoperation module, and operator console. Mitigation: Define QoS policy in Teleoperation; Network Link enforces via standard mechanisms (DSCP, 802.1p).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides strong containment for DP-D. FEC, adaptive encoding, and monitoring are cohesive communication-layer concerns. Interfaces to Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link are clean and well-defined. No significant diffusion expected if boundaries are respected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of packet loss ≤1% is feasible via network simulation, lab testing, and field trials. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics via Telemetry interface for measurement. Acceptance testing protocol should specify network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, loss rate) and measurement duration. Operator and safety manager alerts can be verified via alert log inspection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss mitigation and monitoring. Vehicle module provides telemetry; Safety Services consumes alerts. Network Link provides transport. Responsibilities are non-overlapping and traceable to the FR.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Add a sub-FR for network characterization and acceptance testing protocol. Define how customer-agreed network conditions are measured and validated before deployment.
Revision 2: Specify FEC scheme, overhead budget, and latency impact in DP-D. Include fallback behavior if FEC is not supported by network.
Revision 3: Define packet loss alert thresholds and escalation logic in collaboration with Safety Services. Document in SafetyAlert interface specification.
Revision 4: Add a requirement for operator training on packet loss alerts and graceful degradation procedures.
Revision 5: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Network Link. Define Network Link interface contract for QoS capabilities and fallback behavior.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (error recovery, visibility, graceful degradation). DP-D is sound and well-justified, and Teleoperation is the correct IM boundary with strong containment. However, the sub-FRs require further decomposition to define concrete implementation details (FEC scheme, alert thresholds, fallback logic). Proceeding to the next level will clarify design decisions and reduce implementation risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command delivery.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time packet loss visibility to the operator and safety manager.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert when packet loss exceeds the customer-defined threshold.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and well-characterized.
FEC overhead (increased bandwidth) is acceptable within network capacity.
QoS management is supported by customer's network infrastructure.
Operator training on packet loss alerts is provided.
Adaptive bitrate encoding latency jitter is acceptable for operator control responsiveness.
Teleoperation module runs on hardware with sufficient computational capacity for FEC and monitoring.
Network Link interface provides QoS tagging and packet loss measurement capabilities.
open_risks: Risk: Network conditions may vary significantly, making packet loss targets unachievable. Mitigation: Conduct network characterization and define acceptance testing protocol.
Risk: QoS unavailable in customer's network. Mitigation: Define fallback behavior (e.g., reduce bitrate, alert operator).
Risk: Operator misinterprets packet loss alerts. Mitigation: Provide clear training and documentation.
Risk: Packet loss monitoring adds latency. Mitigation: Implement asynchronously; measure impact in field trials.
Risk: FEC scheme incompatible with network or vehicle firmware. Mitigation: Conduct compatibility testing; use standard FEC schemes (Reed-Solomon, Turbo).
evidence_needed: Network characterization data (latency, jitter, bandwidth, baseline loss rate) for customer's agreed conditions.
FEC scheme selection and overhead analysis (e.g., Reed-Solomon with 10% overhead).
Packet loss alert threshold definition and escalation logic (e.g., alert at 0.5%, escalate to Safety Services at 1%).
Operator console hardware specifications and computational capacity analysis.
QoS mechanism selection and network infrastructure compatibility assessment.
Field trial results demonstrating packet loss ≤1% under agreed conditions.
Operator training materials and competency assessment.
